01-projects/01-_engine/cmake.html.title: CMake
01-projects/01-_engine/cmake.html.description:
I - Roles Purpose
II - Set Up
III - Work Flow
Cmake Tutorial
cmake_minimum_required()
project()
add_executable() this is a documentation of my usage of cmake within my own engine and although it is mostly documented specifically for that purpose it can be used as a generalized case for learning.cmake is an open source, cross-platform family of tools designed to build, test, and package software in an easy to share way Example : although a project made with visual studio can't be rebuilt in a linux system without specific adjustment and version management for each update, with cmake this task can be automized. Initialization : this step is basically the set up of the set up, it will go through building the project in 3 code commands
after initializing a visual studio project with cmake options configure the CMakeList.exe to have these lines : cmake_minimum_required(VERSION "3.10") #sets the minimus version as "3.10" project(project_name VERSION "0.0") #chooses the project name add_executable(project_name main.cxx) #links the project name to the main code to start excuting relevant resources :
cmake_minimum_required()
project()
add_executable() building commands : mkdir [project_build] cd [project_build] cmake ../[project_folder] cmake --build . Debug\[project_name] [Input] brackets are to be ignored and variables within them are to be inserted depending on their name in project Project Version (getting variable from CMake): to get the current project as a variable we must define an input file [project_name]Config.h.in in the project directory and an output file [project_name]Config.h in the build directory one containing commands that defines the versions, while the other contains the version numbers themselves. in tutorialConfig.h.in #define Tutorial_VERSION_MAJOR @Tutorial_VERSION_MAJOR@ #define Tutorial_VERSION_MINOR @Tutorial_VERSION_MINOR@ we must add two lines of code first in the CMakeList.txt one before add_executable() to configure the input and output files and one after to define the binary directory as belowconfigure_file(project_nameConfig.h.in TutorialConfig.h) add_executable(project_name main.cxx) target_include_directories(project_name PUBLIC"${PROJECT_BINARY_DIR}") after that we can include #include "TutorialConfig.h" in main.cxx and now we have access to Tutorial_VERSION_MAJOR and Tutorial_VERSION_MINOR values. Adding a Library : making a library run with CMake requires 2 CMakeList.txt files, one in the project dir and one in the library dir itself.
Lib\CMakeList.txt : add_library(lib_name [STATIC 'OR' SHARED] "included files") difference between static and shared libraries Modern Implementation to Adding Library : Composite Libraries : sometimes we have certain files we only want to compile under certain conditions, for that we have composite libraries where the base files of the lib are compiled then we compile the rest depending on certain variables
usually using a boolean then a if statement is used on that variable as shown : if (BOOLEAN_NAME) target_compile_definitions(BASE_LIB PRIVATE "BOOLEAN_NAME") #defines the boolean as a macro in the base files of the lib add_library(AddLib_Name STATIC AddLib_File.cxx) # additional lib target_link_libraries(MathFunctions PRIVATE SqrtLibrary) #links the 2 libs endif() then within the base file of the lib responsable for the functionality related the added lib we can add a if statement that checks for "BOOLEAN_NAME" as a macro : #include "BaseMib.h" #ifdef BOOLEAN_NAME # include "addLib.h" #endif . . . #ifdef BOOLEAN_NAME return "lib used" #else return "lib not used" #endif Definitions, Properties & Other Notes : a CMakeList.txt is the base of every CMake project, it is the list of all essential commands to be run on project building to ensure proper working version on all specified platforms PUBLIC ,PRIVATE & INTERFACE keywords are what specifies accessibility to a certain build directory, PUBLIC : accessible by other build dir and by itself. PRIVATE : accessible only by itself INTERFACE : accessible by other build dir and not itself SHARED & STATIC keywords are specifications to the library dependencies build type, Static : Means the library gets compiled into a .lib file, they are stored within the binary making them bigger in size and need to create a duplicate of the code for each program that requires it but they do not need special linking in the project and are easier for sharing. Shared : Means the library gets compiled into a .dll file, more compact and light weight, stored in the respective library folder and should be shipped with it, need linking and careful placement but is overall more efficient. Declaring booleans : option(VARIBLE_NAME "a desciption" [ON/OFF]) this is a boolean declaration in CMake and can be changed before the build is created by : cmake ../[Project_dir] -DUSE_[VARIBLE_NAME]=[OFF/ON] Declaring Variables : a variable cannot be independently declared, although it can be added to a list, so a way around this is creating a single element list, by using list() function is CMake : list(APPEND [LIST_NAME] [VALUE])
APPEND append belongs to one of 3 families of keywords that specify what the modification that will happen to the list, append specifically creates a new list.
CMAKE_CXX_KNOWN_FEATURES a collection of c++ compile features that define certain behaviour the compiler should make, like minimum version (EX:cxx_std_11), or define features in individual compiler versions. [under Construction]

02-self-learning/0-computer-science/algo.title: Complexity
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/algo.description:
Sections : I - Time Complexity
II - Space Complexity Time complexity represents an estimate of how long a certain function will take to execute.Big O notation as in O(n) is what we use to represent the time complexity of a function. n indicates the time complexity ranging from 1 → !n from best to worst case, the hierarchy being :O(1) being the the best and shortest complexity and O(n^n) being the worst but very unlikely to happen, making O(!n) basically the worst outcome. Breakdown & Examples : O(1) : → occurs when the time complexity is fixed and doesn't change by changing the value of n. Example : function print(int n){ print(n) //has a fixed excution time. } O(n): → when the time is dependent to one degree on the value of n function loop(int n){ for(int i; i < n; i++){ // time is dependant on value of n. print(n) //has fixed time. } } O(log n): → O(n²): → when the complexity is dependent to 2 degrees on the value of n. function loop(int n){ for(int j; j < n; j++) {// TC is dependant on value of n. for(int i; i < n; i++){ // TC is dependant on value of n. print(n) //has fixed time. } } } O(n^c): → same as one above but c is proportional to the number of loops. O(c^n): → when the complexity is an exponent of n. function fib(int n){ if(n == 1){return 1;} if(n == 0){return 0;} return fib(n-1)+fib(n-2); }
O(!n): → O(n^n): → what are the factors that affect Time complexity? these include : Operations Comparisons loops Pointer Refs function calls to outside space complexity works in the same way but it just accounts for what affects space. "A rule of thumb is that the bigger the time complexity is the smaller the Space complexity and vice versa." mewhat are the factors that affect Space complexity? these include : Declarations (includes parameters) Allocations function calls O(1) Example : bool pair(int n){ int x = 1; int y = 0; return n % 2 == x ; } O(n) Example : int mult(int n){ for(int i = 0; i > n; i++){ int a = n * i; } return a; } -O(n²)
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/algo.author: Vicoodi

02-self-learning/0-computer-science/algo.title: Iterative Types
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/algo.description:
Sections : I - Array
II - Linked-List An array is the most basic iterative type and at the same time the one where the most variations are derived from, the concept is simple as it's just multiple elements each marked with an index. Structural Properties : Functional Properties : Set : Get : Insert : Delete : Change Size : Lists : Multi-Dimension Array : Stacks : Queues : A linked is a connected array of nodes, with each nodes having the data inside the node itself and pointer to the next node in the list. A liked-list is technically a variation of a array as most iterative types are, although it's such a complexe topic it deserves it's own header. Create : some languages come pre-built with List-Node support but even in ones that don't (like c++) creating them is simple requiring at a minimum a struct with 2 members. struct ListNode { //members int data; //data element ListNode *next; //pointer to next node //Constructors ListNode() : val(NULL), next(nullptr) {} ListNode(int x) : val(x), next(nullptr) {} ListNode(int x, ListNode *next) : val(x), next(next) {} }; Get : to get a specific element you must iterate the entier list until you find you're target element, having at worst a TC of O(n). int Find(int target, ListNode* head){ while(head != nullptr && head->data != target){ head = head->next; } return head->data; //returns NULL if target doesn't exist } Assign : assign a value to a node at a certain index in the tree. requires iterating through at most the entier node list until reaching a target index making it's TC O(n) void Assign(int val, int index, ListNode* head){ int i = 0; while(head != nullptr && index != i){ head = head->next; i++; } if(head != nullptr){ head->data = val; } else{ cout<<"index out of range!"<<endl; } } Insert : Insert a specific value at a certain index in the node list 52481130node to be inserted :Frame 01initial NodeListindex to insert in : `3`index5248113initial NodeList0insert a new node into a Listnode the desired result can be achieved by iterating until reaching the target index then storing the node-to-be-replaced in a dummy that keeps permutating while iterating till the end of the nodeList. TC is O(n) void inset(ListNode* head, int index, int to_insert){ ListNode* iterator = head; int i = 0; while(iterator-next && i < index){ iterator = iterator->next; i++; } ListNode* temp; while(iterator){ temp = iterator; iterator.data = to_insert; iterator = iterator->next; to_insert = temp->data; } } 52481130node to be inserted :Frame 01initial NodeListindex to insert in : `3`index5248113initial NodeList0 Erase : Erasing in a Linked List is different than a normal iterative type, there is no built in function for it in it has the risk of leaving garbage memory if not handled well. It is achieved by 3 methods depending on the position of the node-to-delete in the node list. Delete from first node : simply set head = head->next and free the data one the to-be-deleted adresse. TC is O(1) void erase_from-first(ListNode* head){ temp = head; head = head->next; delete temp; //or free(temp) } Delete from last node : iterate a dummy towards the end then free it's memory it another method is to iterate the head itself until the end then override the last value and then freeing first node. TC is O(n) void erase_from-last1(ListNode* head){ temp = head; while(temp->next){ temp = temp->next; } delete temp; //or free(temp) } void erase_from-last2(ListNode* head){ temp = head; head = head->next; delete temp; //or free(temp) while(head->next){ head = head->next; } } Delete from a middle node : iterate with a dummy until reaching the desired index then set iterator->next = iterator->next->next; then make sure to free the unused memory. TC is O(n) void erase_from-midlle1(ListNode* head, int index){ ListNode* iterator = head; for(int i = 0; i < index; i++){ iterator = temp->next; } ListNode* temp = iterator-next; iterator->next = iterator->next->next; delete temp; } Singly Linked-List (default): is the base version of a linked-list were each node has only a pointer to the next node :
Double Linked-List : the double version were each node contains a pointer to the next node and a pointer to the previous node.
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/algo.author: Vicoodi

02-self-learning/0-computer-science/algo.title: Pointers
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/algo.description:
Sections : I - Introduction
II - Concept
III - Application Every data type has a corresponding pointer type EX : int ⇒ *int such special data type is used to store the addressee of a variable stored in memory. aka a pre-declared variablePointers store the addressee of data. Essentially pointers deal with the storing of data within the RAM. Referenced data has variable size depending on the type and content of said data. The RAM is divided into blocks,each block is worth one bit and has an addressee, data can be stored in 1 or more blocks depending on it's size and type. The RamP1P2P3P1 :P2 :P3 :A pointer to bool at the address (00)containing the variable `Test` with a value '0'A pointer to a array of booleansat the address (10) of size 5XyA pointer to an integer at the address (11)containing the variable 'number' with the value '4'YXpointers usually have uncomprehensible addressesit's pointless to try and rationallize it.this is merely an exampletakes up 1 bittakes up 5 bitstakes up 4 bitsIn the context of C list of variables associated with pointers : * : pointer's or de-pointer's a variable malloc(int size) : allocates size amount of bits in the memory to a returned pointer. char[] * ptr = (char*) malloc ( sizeof(char) * nbr_of_char); free(* pointer) : free's up / deletes a pointer char[] * ptr = malloc ( sizeof(char) * nbr_of_char);

02-self-learning/0-computer-science/logi.title: KARNAUGH Map
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/logi.description:
Video [I - Definition]
when needing to simplify a boolean equation, we can use a Karnaugh Map or a K-Map. created by Maurice Karnaugh. this is concise, easy and reliable method to create boolean formulas.The process for such method is quite situation dependent, thus we will start with an example, from this Truth Table :for a concise solution a table as big as this is not gonna do, especially in code where a general answer that works for all circumstances is needed, thus we must contain all this in one algebraic boolean expressionKarnaugh Map Conversion : the process of converting a truth table to a k-map is quite loose. we start by selecting which value(s) will represent the columns and which ones will represent the rows. (in this case CD:col; AB:row). Note : the number cols or rows can be uneven after that in the intersection of each mini equation we put the result, getting a result similar to what's below Formula Extraction : with the creation of the k-map out of the way we can get to selection (circling), which is the process of selecting 1 values by circling them, one simple rule is to be followed thou, you are only allowed to circle a n number of values, with n having to be a number to the power of 2 (no 3,5,6,..) As you can see there is no restriction on vertical or horizontal selection or even square circling's. from this we can derive the formula: we take one circling at a time for example we'll take we start by inspecting the variable combinations associated with it and on the rows and *on the cols seeing which variables are fixed and which ones change Between and values value changes but that doesn't stop the result from being 1 in both rows, Meanwhile value is constant as is the result. As for we must take it as a whole. thus we derive three variables vital for the formula to be true : and So for Circle 1 : repeating this process for each selection we get Combining all circles we get Note : to get a K-Map from a formula simply reversing the process is all takes

02-self-learning/0-computer-science/low-.title: Assembly
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/low-.description:
good course with sufficient material
good article
I - Introduction
II - Operations
III - Memory Management
VI - Generic Structures basic level understanding of assembly pass my exam
assembly is an ensemble of programming languages that represent machine code instructions as human readable code, which makes them the closest in nature to machine code, the first recorded use of this language was found in Kathleen and Andrew Donald Booth's 1947 work, [Coding for A.R.C](Kathleen and Andrew Donald Booth's 1947 work, Coding for A.R.C) unlike other programming languages, assembly stores it's variables not in RAM but in the CPU in designated 32-bit registries. most of these variables are prefixed like AX: the accumulator, you can name your own variable but we'll be sticking with the standard variable names for this Documentation. most notable variables : AX: the Primary Accumulator used mainly to preform arithmetic operations. BX: Base Register used for storing data. CX: Count Register reserved. used for storing loop count's in iterations. DX: Data Register
half-reserved more... used for storing data. To be honest, although these Variables are what you would be using when programming assembly, it is actually not the full 32-bit register as I mentioned. the Actual full register names are EAX, EBX, ECX and EDX. for the purpose of this Doc, we only deal with data less then 32-bit, thus we don't need the full register. to access a variables pointer, simply put the variable name in [], ex: [AX] Note : even with in these sub-registers we have further sub-sub-register
These sub-registers are mainly hold-overs from older, 16-bit versions of the instruction set. However, they are sometimes convenient when dealing with data that are smaller than 32-bits (e.g. 1-byte ASCII characters). source->1
Note : CPU's with a 64-bit architecture have bigger registers for example : EAX would be a sub-register in the full 64-bit register RAX. Note : the EDX register is said to be half-reserved because any results from operations preformed on the EAX that surpass 32-bit have their excess stored in 16-bits from EAX and 16-bits from EDX. for simplicity's sake we'll put some ground rules. all operations take one line : Variables Allowed for use are Ax, BX and DX. all boiler plate is unessential. MSW : refers to the machine status word, which is a registry that stores the results of some operations (like CMP) and gives the input to some instructions (like JMP<conditional>), due it's nature it must be used as an output right after being given an input. any Operation works in this form : [OPERATION] <OP1>, <OP2> OR [OPERATION] <OP>, Thus with that in mind the operations we'll be focusing in are these. Operations for use : MOV : copies the value from OP2 to OP1.
PUSH : Pushes OP to the top of the memory stack
POP : Pops the last element of the memory stack into OP ADD : Adds OP2 to OP1 SUB : Subtracts OP2 from OP1 MUL : Multiplies OP1 by OP2 DIV : Divides OP1 by OP2 CMP : Compares between OP1 and OP2 and stores the result in MSW like any programming language assembly holds conditional structures, like <if>..<else>, <while> etc.. Conditional structures : JMP : "jumps" towards OP (aka it's a goto function) <conditional>JMP : the name of this condition changes with each intended use, but it simply preforms an JMP if the condition in MSW is met, it's variants are : je : (jump when equal) jne :(jump when not equal) jz : (jump when last result was zero) jg : (jump when greater than) jge :(jump when greater than or equal to) jl : (jump when less than) jle :(jump when less than or equal to) Note : the memory stack is the allocated amount of memory to the program, (usually from the RAM) as the name suggest the memory is "stacked" atop itself in a LIFO form (Last in,First out) aka: push puts AX as the last in the stack and when pop take AX as the first out. if(condition)then{};else{} CMP AX, 3 JE THEN JMP ELSE THEN : ;instruction here ELSE : ;instruction here for(iterator)do{} MOV CX, 5; CX the register reserved for loops ;and 5 as the number of iterations START_LOOP : ;instruction here ;CX decrements automatically LOOP START_LOOP etc..
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/low-.author: Vicoodi

02-self-learning/0-computer-science/low-.title: Computer Architecture
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/low-.description:
wiki
Introduction
Bus gain an understanding of how a computer architecture understand theoretical concepts comprehend low level practices
A microprocessor is effectively a CPU, thus any machinewith a microprocessor is a computer. there are two defining features of a microprocessor that separates it from a micro controller and such : it contains complicated circuits capable of preforming arithmetic operations it contains registers for fast storage and access of data other properties it that are not exclusive to it are :
a clock defined by a clock speed (in Hz)
microprocessors can only understand machine code which is simply organizing bits in certain patterns to execute commands and instructions, due to this being too complex for humans a bridge was made, Assembly A microprocessor is mainly made up of 2 units The Arithmetic Logic Unit : used to preform arithmetic and logic operations. The Control Unit : used to execute instructions and command other units. In addition to the 2 units a microprocessor possesses a General Use Registry it holds all registers used for storing fast access data. Note : the term micro is only attached because computers used to be the size of a house, thus the original processor was a lot bigger then the modern thus a new label was given to at the time more compact processors to make a distinction.^227af1 Note :
the clock is an internal component (external in other cases), that gives time, used by all programs in the computer. it's clock speed is indicative of how many calls the CPU can make in a second (4 GHz, 3.5 GHz, etc), this component is so important that any slight issue in it's settings can cause a global problem (check Y2K) a Bus or a data high way is concept with hardware and software intersection, it studies the optimal and diverse methods to align cables circuits in a way that guarantees smooth and orderly for data transfer and storage. we'll be mainly discussing the busses connecting the CPU to the RAM
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/low-.author: Vicoodi

02-self-learning/0-computer-science/low-.title: Operating Systems
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/low-.description:
Sources : () Sections :
I - Introduction : operating systems are..do you seriously not know what an OS is?In a Hard Drive Disc, at the lowest level data is managed by dividing it into sector of bytes, each sector clustered into clusters (see what i did there :D).At the initialization of the OS, it is decided how many sectors a cluster has (by default value or by the user). this action very much effects how files are stored and treated by the OS.
this manifest in files with higher cluster size being stored more elegantly, (as in a single file is usually stored in a single cluster) contrary to being divided into multiple sectors (that aren't necessarily stored next to each other) in essence : clusters with more sectors lead to more performance, yet less storage clusters with less sectors provide more storage, yet is more taxing on the CPU.
02-self-learning/0-computer-science/low-.author: Vicoodi

02-self-learning/2-math/euler.html.title: Euler
02-self-learning/2-math/euler.html.description:
Sections : I - The Euler System The Euler system is composed of 3 numbers (a matrix is more accurate than a vector) all real used to represent rotation in a 3D or 2D space. for Example : Rotation :XYZ90°45°0°XYZYXZ*order of rotation matters euler has 3 axis that can be rotated upon individually to form any angle. this rotation is sometimes heavily effected by the order of application and in some cases the final results may even differ to solve this issue a new technique was created within the same system, Gimbals which basically consists of parenting the axis to each other (usually XYZ) although a problem can still arise from this.. Gimbal Lock :
a Gimbal lock is what happens when upon rotation 2 Gimbals control the same axis. effectively this restricts movement in one axis making Euler a system that is not effective in 100% of all situations. one might what is the solution to this problem?
⇒ answer is don't use euler, instead use Quaternions “Recording 20251016080413.webm” could not be found.

02-self-learning/2-math/integrals.html.description:
Sources : Docs
Vid Sections :
I - Definition
II - Practicality
III - Theoretic the foul snake.. also known as a way to calculate area underneath a curve. the concept maybe hard to grasp on mathematical level but it essentially needs 2 values and calculating the derivative of one function.Area00.20.40.60.811.21.41.61.822.22.42.62.8300.20.40.60.811.21.41.61.82TimeVelocity with the shown above graph, within a given time, of 2s starting from t=0, to t=2; with a changing acceleration we must calculate the area beneath the curve to get distance moved. calculating changing velocity using means is almost impossible thus we simplify this process and are contempt with simply approximating the area, as such we start by dividing the curve into sections(as small as possible) in rectangle shapes which we can calculate, the first step would be dictating a number as small as possible denoted , this will be the difference between each anchor point we choose and also the width of each rectangle. then with the formula : we can the area of one section. by calculating the sum of all these sections we get an approximation of the area beneath the curve. we can organize all these info into a nice yet terrifying SUM this notation is technically correct which is expanded on below, but it is preferred to use the specific integral notation to describe area. the primary difference between the practical and theoretical approach is the use of instead of , although the sum notation may apply when speaking practically since we're are adding up sums of rectangles to approximate a area, in the theoretical it becomes less of a practical sum and more of a concrete value, that is approximated using thus we change the notation

02-self-learning/2-math/matrix.html.title: Matrix
02-self-learning/2-math/matrix.html.description:
Sections : I - A Matrix
II - Properties
III - Matrix Methods Sources :
Great Breakdown of main concepts Matrix is a rectangular array of numbers, symbols, points, or characters each belonging to a specific row and column. Example : both (+) and (-) operation can happen in Matrices on element per element basis. Example : as for (*) operation it requires a more complicated approach, Equation : Note : ==A + B = B + A == (A + B) + C = A + (B + C) AB != BA (AB) C = A (BC) A (B+C) = AB + AC there are multiple types of matrices each with unique properties, square matrices : equal columns and rows Rectangular matrices : unequal columns and rows Row matrices & column matrices : one row/column matrix etc... a determinant is a number associated with a square matrix, symbolized by or . it can be used to inverse the matrix if it's value is none zero. Equation :
Note : the 2x2 matrix used in the multiplication of or or is called the minor of said element, where is Minor of element is a 2x2 matrix made by deleting the row and column of the in reference element. it is usually denoted as a cofactor is a property of an element of a matrix, denoted as or and is defined as follows : where is : is the cofactor value is the minor of element Example : for :$$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 5&3&1\2&0&-1\1&2&3 \end{bmatrix} C_{32} = (-1)^{3+2} . \begin{bmatrix} 5&3\\1&2 \end{bmatrix} $$ thus a adjoint is a modification of a matrix used to calculate other aspects of a matrices like their inverse. Formula for a 2x2 matrix : for : $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \ c & d \end{bmatrix} adj(A) = \begin{bmatrix} d & -c \ -b & a \end{bmatrix} $$ Formula for a 3x3 matrix : for : replace each element to it's respective cofactor then we just transpose the matrix(which means to flip it) : for a matrix with a non zero determinant, we can have a inverse matrix of called . the inverse of is only when : ; a good indication if a matrix is inversible or not is the determinant, if , but even then a inverse may not exist. for a Matrix we have as it's inverse with the formula : a matrix may have some unknown values, and there are many methods to solving for these variables. some exceptionell cases need to be known to handle these methods Binomial Formula : Newton's Binomial Formula is applicable on 2 Matrices and if and only if : and Triangular Matrix : a triangular matrix is one in the form of: it is basically a triangle of zero's, this is very important because it facilitates solving any equation pertaining to systems or matrices. Equation System : an equation system is a collection of equations that share the same variables. making their solutions related to some capacity. EX : in some sense they are related to matrices, as any matrix with unknown variables can be represented in system form and all systems can be represented as matrices, this fundamentally makes all matrix theorems applicable on systems giving a lot more solutions to solving them. Cramer Method : another resolution method that relies on simple yet effective math to get a straightforward solution, no exceptions or weird rules, that's why It's my favorite method (plus the funny name) For a matrix A with 3 unknowns (x, y, z) with a result of (a, b, c) to get any of the 3 variables a simple formula is made this may look daunting at first but the principle is to divide the matrix into 3 columns (x, y, z) then replacing the column representing our desired variable with the solution (a ,b ,c) respectfully in each row. repeating this for each variable yields the solution Gaussian elimination :
Gaussian elimination is a methide to solve a system that relies on eliminating variables from the equations to create a Triangular Matrix this involves doing operation between rows until we get the desired result; EX : we will keep as a pivot used to eliminate the x variable from other rowswe will keep as the pivot to remove the variable from the last rowwith this replacing we know that which we can replace in the second row, yielding the result of then repeat the process for the first row.
02-self-learning/2-math/matrix.html.author: Vicoodi

02-self-learning/2-math/numeric-bases.ht.title: Numeric Bases
02-self-learning/2-math/numeric-bases.ht.description:
Sections : I - Introduction
II - Conversion between numeric systems :
III - numeric systems are bases for numbering systems we have, the base 10 system is commonly used (probably due to humans having 10 fingers and ease of calculation), but there exists a infinity of other numeration systems. most basic of them all is base-2 e.i 0 and 1 most influential numeric systems : binary(0,1) Octal(0,...,8) Decimal(0,...,9) Hexadecimal(0,...,9,A,...,F) Decimal Binary : to convert a decimal number to a binary one we use a algorithm that consists of doing Euclidean division on by then repeating the process on the rest until we're left with a rest of Example :450222221(4)_10 = (100)_2(50)_10 = (110010)_2222222512631for x = 4for x = 50 function B10_B2(int x) { ch = "" while(x > 0){ ch = str(x % 2) + ch x = x // 2 } return ch } Binary Decimal : binary to decimal conversion is slightly easier as it has a pseudo-formula, for a binary number to be converted to a decimal one we use Example : for for function B2_B10(ch:chain): Debut x ← 0;p ← 1 pour i de n-1 à 0 (pas-1) faire x ← x + valeur(ch[i])*p p ← p * 2 Fin Decimal Any : ``` fonction B10_B(x,b:entier) Début Hex = "0123456789ABCDEF" ch = "" tantque(x > 0)faire ch ← Convch(pos(x mod b, hex))+ch x ← x div b fintq retourner ch Fin - **Any $\to$ Decimal :** ``` fonction B2_B10(ch:chain, b:entier): Debut hex ← "0123456789ABCDEF" x ← 0;p ← 1 pour i de n-1 à 0 (pas-1) faire x ← x + valeur(hex.find(ch[i]))*p p ← p * b return x Fin binary is a very important base as it's the smallest one representing only a state of 1 or 0; this it is the one commonly used by computers. with that there are multiple variations on how to represent binary and how to interpret it. it is the norm and the one we usually use in most conversions, this is the natural representation of binary intended by god, all other are heresy that we must eradicate... BCD is one of the most well known binary encoding classes for decimal numbers, it is very similar to natural Binary in that it takes the same approach in conversion with the exception that each decimal digit has a dedicated 4-bits (4 digit) binary representation for Example : Note with this approach we can use the four bits to represent each decimal digit then an additional digit to represent the sign EX : since BCD represents the binary value of each digit individually conversion between it and decimal would be quiet simple. Formula : Gray Code also known as reflected binary code (RBC) is another class of binary decoding, it is widely used to prevent spurious output from electromechanical switches and to facilitate error correction in digital communications such as digital terrestrial television and some cable TV systems, The use of Gray code in these devices helps simplify logic operations and reduce errors in practice. the advantage µGRAY holds over other variations of Binary code is the fact that GRAY only changes one digit between each 2 consecutive numbers EX : because of this an interesting phenomenon occurs in the GRAY table namely some all rows that share the same number of bits are mirrored. EX : [0] & [1] are mirrored in 1-bits, [0,1] & [2,3] are mirrored in 1-bit, ..., [0,.., 7] & [8, .., F] are mirrored in 3-bit, ect Binary and Gray Conversion : Binary GRAY Conversion : this conversion starts off by ignoring the first left most bit from the binary number and filling each bit of the GRAY number by the result of the XOR comparason between n-bit and n+1-bit till the end. in layman's terms this works by first ignoring the last digit, then XOR'ing our way through all bits. 011101XORXORXORXOR1011GRAY:Binary: GRAY Binary Conversion : this conversion works in a slightly similar manner. first we ignore the first digit of GRAY and put it as is in Bin, then we compare between i-bit of Bin and (i+1)-bit of GRAY with XOR, 011101XORXORXORXOR1011GRAY:Binary:

02-self-learning/2-math/quaternions.html.title: Quaternions
02-self-learning/2-math/quaternions.html.description:
Sections : I - Definition
II - Euler System
III - Quaternions, a Higher Dimension System Sources :
Visualizing quaternions
Euler (gimbal lock) Explained Quaternions are a system composed of 4 numbers, 3 of them being imaginary numbers that represent an axis and one real number representing a angle. this system is usually used in 3D space for rotation calculation specifically to solve an issue know as gimble lock(while plane imaginary numbers are used in 2D space for rotation) the biggest difference between quaternions and euler is that that a quaternion possesses 4 axis instead of 3.

headers.heading: III - Quaternions, a Higher Dimension System :

02-self-learning/2-math/trigonometry.htm.title: Trigonometry
02-self-learning/2-math/trigonometry.htm.description:
Sources : source on Trig Functions
interactive unit circle
cheat sheet Sections :
I - Basics
II - Unit Circle
III - Trigonometric relations
IV - Inverse Trigonometric Functions
V - Derivatives & Limits To Add : Pythagorean theorem triangles derivatives limits other theorems (sin(a+b) ,etc..) Trigonometric functions are a branch of mathematics used to calculate relations between triangles (from which we derive important math used to calculate circles, waves and looping concepts) used VERY heavily in Computer science fields (especially game development ;) ). the 3 Trigonometric functions are : Also known as the circle defined by a very crucial piece of info to understanding these functions is the unit circle. Conditions for a circle to be unit circle : Radius = 1 Origin at O(0, 0) any point in the circumference of the unit circle would have a angle relative to the origin point O, starting with the right most point being at angle 0 (cos(0) = 1, sin(0) = 0). in other words any point with an angle can be found by searching . thus we say that the trigonometric functions are used to specify points on this circle.
“Pasted image 20251209161121.png” could not be found. due to the nature of a circle, values cycle. this is to say that and are the same value, the same applies to [ and ] and even counter clock wise [ and ] even though this lesson is called trigonometry we haven't encountered a single triangle yet. this is not because we've been lied to, but simply because i believe the concept is better represented with circle rather than triangles, this is not to say one is better then the other, but that within the unit circle the relation between sin, cos and tan can be better represented through triangles. “2. Self-Learning/2. Math/Drawing 2025-12-09 19.12.09.excalidraw.png” could not be found. aside from explaining the shape presenting all functions, there are more formulas that elaborate on their relation. but first we must understand the below module : “Pasted image 20251209195210.png” could not be found. We conclude the following relations from the image: Note : the naming of the sides is derived from each side's relation to the angle x aka : Opposite to the angle x, Adjacent to the angle x and hypotenuse is just doing it's own thing with out knowledge of the previous graph we can equate these relations to : (look this up, doubted to be wrong it just doesn't make sense) Note : there are many theorems for Trigonometric family that your head might burst, but going from simplest to and most useful to most complicated and less used are : Pythagorean Theorem : technically speaking the Pythagorean theorem is not part of the trigonometric family, but I put it here because they are usually though of and used in tandem, it states that and by proxy that : Pythagorean Identities : Addition Formulas : classic trigonometric functions yield a position from an angle. yet what if we know the position but not the angle? the solution is the inverse function. Inverse Functions : For a function which returns a y value upon receiving the x coordinate, it's inverse provides the x when given the y. That is to say; example : when this concept is applied to Trigonometric functions we get : Note : all inverse functions, work by relying on the fact that it's reading the upper right side of the unit circle, but in fact the desired output can be the opposite or any other value, (since there are a infinite variation of angles to be given, that often times share the same value, ). it is up to us to remember there can be other answers -mathisfun

03-software/blender/dithering.html.title: Dithering
03-software/blender/dithering.html.description:
I - Definition
Types
Usage and Implementation
https://surma.dev/things/ditherpunk/ great light hearted article
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ordered_dithering
https://spencerszabados.github.io/blog/2022/bayer-dithering/ a god send
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9zsaegcL_84 great vid dithering is a rendering technique that stimulates gradients while using less colors. i.e.: using a smaller color palette to represent an image without loosing on details and extra shades, this relay's on the human brain's ability to fill in the gaps, in turn this results in (in some cases) a more stimulating imagethere is no practical benefit to dithering in our modern age thus you would usually see this technique used in entertainment to entice a feeling of nostalgia and retro atmosphere notably in video games wink wink thus you might notice that accompanied by the delightful dither there is a significant drop in resolution and in turn some loss of detail. this is however is intentional to allow the dithering to be more noticeable. there are many types of dithering from bayer/ordered dithering to blue noise dithering, etc..., but we'll only be focusing on bayer (because I like it). Global to all types of dithering is the concept of quantization, it is the act of limiting the color palette to allow the dithering to do it's work (this is unnecessary for old images that have a smaller color gradient and is purely intended for modern images that dithering was not intended for to begin with) we can technically dither and image without quantizing it first but the results would be bland. Quantization is technically a compression method although it has been seeing a decline over the years it can still be seen as artifact from over-compression in image formats like .jpeg we'll be referring to the number of quantized color values as c we'll only be covering the bayer dithering method
Bayer dithering make use of a matrix of threshold values that are compared to each pixel value in the already quantized image, relacing the pixel value by the result of the comparison. More specifically, for a matrix , the greyscale values of each pixel, of , are quantized to number of values based on the rule: Bayer's Matrix : out of all the confusing terms in this article one term might stand out, which is matrix of threshold values, to put it simply this is the matrix used to dictate the placement of the dots in the most optimal way (in other words magic), this is what allows the creation of patterns like these

p.s.: you might notice a small difference between, the 2 images, this is dues to the second having gamma correction while the first doesn't the Bayer threshold matrices are recursive meaning that it can be as big or as small as we need it to as long as we manage to calculate it correctly, it's terms go as follow:
although the values may seem nonsensical on a much smaller scale it is a lot more obvious once applied: Gamma Correction :
due to a difference in color spaces and the human eye not seeing brightness linearly, in some cases some values might appear brighter then they originally are. “The human eye responds to low light levels by enlarging the pupil, allowing more light to enter the eye. This response results in a difference between measured and perceived light levels” example : for a color value considering that is black and is white, you might think that is true grey yet for the human eye it looks a lot brighter. the equation for this is :

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
